Params: {2: 'Elysium, night, inside Moore and Elean\'s apartment.'}
{3: 'The room is bathed in the soft, amber glow of pendant lights, their light reflecting off rich wooden surfaces.'}
{1: '1', 3: 'Elean places the final dish on the dining table, her gaze sweeping over the carefully placed settings. A soft sigh escapes her.'}
{3: 'They should be here any minute now, she thinks to herself.'}
{3: 'As if summoned by the thought, the lock clicks. The door swings open, and Moore steps inside, ushering in Teddy and Moineau.'}
{3: 'Moore shrugs off his coat and finds its place on the entryway rack.'}
{1: '1', 2: 'Moore', 3: 'Make yourselves at home. Seriously, no need for formalities.', 4: '1'}
{1: '1', 3: 'Teddy cuts a sharp glance toward Moore before her demeanor shifts seamlessly into one of perfect poise, extending a hand to Elean.'}
{2: 'Teddy', 3: 'I\'m Christina Norman. But just Teddy is fine.', 4: '3'}
{2: 'Elean', 3: 'It\'s lovely to meet you. Thank you for looking after Moore at work.', 4: '15'}
{3: 'Elean extends her hand to Moineau, her expression visible with nervousness.'}
{1: '1', 3: 'Once the formal handshakes conclude, Moore steps in, softening the tension with a gentle, reassuring hug.'}
{2: 'Moore', 3: 'Hey, why so tense? They\'re just my friends. Nothing to worry about.', 4: '5'}
{3: 'With that, Moore leads the way to the dining table.'}
{3: 'They settle into a stiff, silent circle, the air thick with unspoken words.'}
{1: '1', 3: 'He fills the void by switching on the television—the evening news chattering brightly—before turning his full attention back to Teddy and Moineau.'}
{1: '1', 2: 'Moore', 3: 'So, I heard you two knew each other before you became partners at the bureau?', 4: '1'}
{1: '1', 2: 'Moore', 3: 'Just some office gossip. You know how it is.', 4: '1'}
{2: 'Moore', 3: 'Figured you two might not hear much of it. You both keep to yourselves more than most.', 4: '1'}
{2: 'Moore', 3: 'Could you pass the mashed potatoes, please?', 4: '1'}
{1: '1', 3: 'Moineau passes the dish of mashed potatoes to him.'}
{2: 'Moineau', 3: 'That\'s correct. Teddy and I go way back.', 4: '3'}
{2: 'Moineau', 3: 'Back then, she was a rather melancholy girl.', 4: '3'}
{1: '1', 3: 'Teddy shoots a sharp glare at Moore.'}
{1: '1', 2: 'Teddy', 3: 'And here I thought you were the bureau\'s resident nice guy...', 4: '2'}
{2: 'Teddy', 3: 'Why does everything lately remind me of that annoying man...', 4: '2'}
{1: '1', 2: 'Leonardo', 3: 'I just want to bond with my little sister. You know, I don\'t often have the chance!', 4: '2'}
{2: 'Leonardo', 3: 'Woah, is that all you\'ve got to say to your brother?', 4: '2'}
{3: 'Teddy lets out a quiet sigh, her knife slicing into the steak with sudden, pointed force.'}
{1: '1', 3: 'Under the table, Moore stifles a wince as Elean\'s shoe presses firmly onto his foot.'}
{3: 'Moineau glances around at the houseplants and flowers decorating the room.'}
{3: 'Elean startles at the conversation\'s sudden pivot toward her, reacting like a student caught daydreaming by a teacher\'s pointed question.'}
{1: '1', 2: 'Teddy', 3: 'It\'s a good hobby to have. The vegetation coverage in this city is at an appalling low, after all.', 4: '2'}
{2: 'Moore', 3: 'Right? Who knows where all of Elysium\'s tax money actually goes?', 4: '1'}
{3: 'Like a pre-written script executing, Moore instinctively fills the silence, keeping the conversation moving.'}
{1: '1', 2: 'Elean', 3: 'I just remembered I still have some plants that need watering...', 4: '2'}
{3: 'Elean rises from her seat with an apologetic look.'}
{3: 'Elean doesn\'t answer. Instead, she simply turns and carries a plant from the living room corner out to the balcony.'}
{1: '1', 3: 'As she passes, Teddy\'s gaze catches on a familiar-looking yellow-orange cup sitting on the windowsill. It\'s filled with soil, cradling a single, fiery orange-red flower.', 4: '2'}
{1: '1', 2: 'Moore', 3: 'The Jack case? I\'ve got some leads.', 4: '1'}

{2: 'Moore', 3: 'His record was spotless. He moved to Elysium five years ago and opened a high-end restaurant. The place has professional management, strong performance, and stable finances.', 4: '1'}
{2: 'Moore', 3: 'He was on a dating site. Dated three women. Two were one-time dinner meetings. The third, he met twice, but it didn\'t go anywhere.', 4: '1'}
{2: 'Moore', 3: 'I did field interviews today. Didn\'t turn up much.', 4: '1'}

{2: 'Moore', 3: 'The consensus is: "He\'s a decent guy, but too old-fashioned. Quiet. Honestly, a bit boring. Can you believe his favorite movie is The Terminating Robot? A story about a robot from the future sent back to assassinate the mother of humanity\'s future leader."', 4: '1'}
{2: 'Moore', 3: 'I guess that old-school type isn\'t really in style anymore.', 4: '1'}
{1: '1', 2: 'Moore', 3: 'I searched his residence. His apartment is exceptionally clean and tidy, clearly well-maintained. For a single man, his habits were remarkably disciplined.', 4: '1'}
{2: 'Moore', 3: 'His fridge is fully stocked, including fresh fruit. Most single guys I know can\'t be bothered; they just take vitamin pills.', 4: '1'}
{2: 'Moore', 3: 'Breakfast at home, lunch at his restaurant, and dinner at home.', 4: '1'}
{2: 'Moore', 3: 'He had a home theater system and always watched a movie after dinner.', 4: '1'}
{2: 'Moore', 3: 'There are even three identical suits and a dedicated tie rack in his closet.', 4: '1'}
{2: 'Moore', 3: 'The guy lived a life of stability that most people can only dream of.', 4: '1'}
{3: 'After saying this, he falls silent for a moment.'}
{2: 'Moore', 3: 'The only anomaly is his diary. He wrote:', 4: '1'}
{1: '"Every time I look into the mirror, I see a man standing on a lone log floating down a river."', 2: '191970', 3: '1', 4: 'Assets/Product/Texture/Image/BgStory/BgStory968.png', 6: '1', 7: '-2'}
{1: '"Our past, our present, and our future."', 2: '191970', 3: '1', 4: 'Assets/Product/Texture/Image/BgStory/BgStory968.png', 6: '1', 7: '1'}
{1: '"All of it is nothing but bubbles."', 2: '191970', 3: '1', 4: 'Assets/Product/Texture/Image/BgStory/BgStory968.png', 6: '1', 7: '1'}
{1: '"If the world is but an illusion, and consciousness just a phantom..."', 2: '191970', 3: '1', 4: 'Assets/Product/Texture/Image/BgStory/BgStory968.png', 6: '1', 9: '1'}
{1: '1', 2: 'Teddy', 3: 'I\'ve been thinking... Rioting machines appeared in both Christina\'s and Jack\'s cases. There must be some sort of connection between the two.', 4: '2'}
{2: 'Teddy', 3: 'And that connection has to be the key to the killer\'s motive.', 4: '2'}
{1: '1', 2: 'Teddy', 3: 'Both Jack and Christina questioned the nature of reality itself.', 4: '2'}
{1: '1', 2: 'Moineau', 3: 'Are you saying we have a serial killer targeting people who believe the world isn\'t real?', 4: '3'}
{1: '1', 2: 'Moore', 3: '...There\'s another lead. His bank records show a weekly transfer, same time, every week. Payments to a psychological clinic.', 4: '1'}
{2: 'News', 3: 'Welcome to Elysium Trending. Our guest today is a leading expert in psychology, Professor John.'}
{2: 'News', 3: 'Our topic is a reportedly trending condition in our city: patients questioning whether the world around them is real. Is this a genuine phenomenon, or just another urban legend?'}
{3: 'The table falls silent as all three of them turn to look at the TV. Moore turns up the volume.'}

{1: '1', 3: 'On screen, a man sits poised on a sofa. He is impeccably dressed in a single-breasted gray suit with a white coat worn over it, a black-green striped tie perfectly mirroring the color of his eyes. His beard and hair are meticulously groomed.'}
{3: 'He radiates a trustworthy aura, the kind that makes a patient surrender their doubts without a second thought.'}
{2: 'Psychologist John', 3: 'Academically, we refer to this condition as "Derealization Disorder".', 4: '2'}
{2: 'Psychologist John', 3: 'There is a school of thought in my field that posits that deep within the human psyche, there exists a powerful self-destructive tendency.', 4: '2'}
{2: 'Psychologist John', 3: 'Perhaps some of you at home have felt this. When standing at a great height, looking down, a subtle impulse whispers in your mind...', 4: '2'}
{2: 'Psychologist John', 3: 'Or when holding a blade, staring at its edge, a thought flickers...', 4: '2'}
{2: 'Psychologist John', 3: '"What if I use it on myself?"', 4: '2'}
{2: 'Psychologist John', 3: 'Even with a gun in your hand, you might feel a compulsion to turn the barrel inward...', 4: '2'}

{1: '1', 2: 'Psychologist John', 3: 'Getting back to "Derealization Disorder", the patients themselves share no clear commonalities. A successful restaurateur and a dockworker can both fall prey to doubting their reality.', 4: '2'}
{2: 'Psychologist John', 3: 'In fact, setting my professional role aside, I can empathize with the impulse to question the authenticity of our world.', 4: '2'}
{3: 'He flounders, the thread of the conversation slipping through his mental grasp.'}
{1: '1', 2: 'Psychologist John', 3: 'Consider that our conscious mind is merely the co-pilot of the body. So what, then, sits in the driver\'s seat?', 4: '2'}
{2: 'Psychologist John', 3: 'That "pilot" varies. For some, it is "instinct." For others, "emotion," or simply "habit"...', 4: '2'}
{2: 'Psychologist John', 3: 'And from the passenger seat, you look out and see the reason to "jump."', 4: '2'}
{2: 'Psychologist John', 3: 'You see an exquisite world: vast, beautiful, and cruel, operating in endless, intricate cycles.', 4: '2'}
{2: 'Psychologist John', 3: 'Faced with such a spectacle, who wouldn\'t pause to wonder: Is the world I live in truly real?', 4: '2'}
{3: 'The man\'s voice is calm, methodical, laying out a philosophy that borders on heresy.'}
{3: 'With every word, something long buried begins to stir inside you.'}
{3: 'You are in the passenger seat, your neck straining as you fight to look up.'}
{1: '1', 2: '<color=#CD2626><size=80>The truth has always been just one page away</size></color>', 4: '50', 6: '1', 7: '6', 8: '0.8', 11: '1'}
{2: 'Yes. The truth has always been just one page away.'}
{2: 'You\'ve always known, Teddy, that you are...'}
{1: '1', 3: 'The television screen dies, plunging the room into a sudden, ringing silence. Elean stands in the living room, her expression utterly unreadable.'}
{3: 'The dinner gathering dissolves into quiet goodbyes, each guest retreating into the silence of their own thoughts.'}
{3: 'On her way out, Teddy\'s gaze drifts to the living room windowsill. The yellow-orange cup is gone, relocated to the balcony by Elean.'}
{1: '1', 2: 'Teddy', 3: 'What\'s that flower called? The orange-red one on your balcony.', 4: '2'}
{3: 'Teddy turns and leaves, but the image of the cup stays with her, vivid and unresolved.'}
{1: '1', 2: 'Teddy', 3: '(Why do I feel that cup was meant for holding serum?)', 4: '1'}
{3: 'Moore clears the table while Elean washes dishes in the kitchen.'}
{1: '1', 2: 'Moore', 3: 'You can just leave the washing to me.', 4: '1'}
{3: 'Elean doesn\'t respond, leaving the room in an unsettling stillness.'}
{2: 'Elean', 3: 'Why did you choose to become an inspector?', 4: '2'}
{1: '1', 2: 'Psychologist John', 3: 'And from the passenger seat, you look out and see the reason to "jump."', 4: '2'}
{2: 'Psychologist John', 3: 'You see an exquisite world: vast, beautiful, and cruel, operating in endless, intricate cycles.', 4: '2'}
{2: 'Psychologist John', 3: 'Faced with such a spectacle, who wouldn\'t pause to wonder: Is the world I live in truly real?', 4: '2'}
{3: 'The man\'s voice is calm, methodical, laying out a philosophy that borders on heresy.'}
{3: 'With every word, something long buried begins to stir inside you.'}
{3: 'You are in the passenger seat, your neck straining as you fight to look up.'}
{1: '1', 3: 'A woman in a black dress lies in a pool of crimson.'}
{3: 'A porcelain plate slips from Elean\'s grasp, exploding into a constellation of white shards on the dark marble.'}
{1: '1', 3: 'She spins, her eyes wide in shock and fixed on him.'}
{1: '1', 3: 'And in her expression, Moore finally hears his own words.'}
{2: 'Moore', 3: 'No... I didn\'t mean it like that. I was just wondering why Jack would think that way...', 4: '1'}
